summary: DHH argues that dual processor machines are essential not for single-application benchmarks but for real-world multitasking performance. He explains that context switching between tasks is expensive for a single processor, and that having two processors dramatically reduces this overhead. The post advocates for judging processor setups by multitasking capability rather than synthetic benchmarks, using everyday examples like photo indexing, file extraction, and code compilation. He expresses enthusiasm about OS X's ability to intelligently distribute work across processors and hints at an upcoming PowerMac purchase.
key_points: Benchmarks mislead because they test single-threaded workloads, not real-world multitasking
Context switching is the hidden cost that makes single-processor machines feel slow under load
Dual processors shine during concurrent everyday tasks like compiling, extracting files, and indexing photos
The OS's intelligence in distributing work across processors is key to realizing dual-CPU benefits
money_quotes: That's when a dual setup will shine.
It's very expensive for a processor to switch tasks.
When you just have a single processor and 50 active tasks screaming for a slice of the silicon, it's going to spend the majority of it's resources simply switching back and forth between the tasks.
That's a very ineffective mode of operation that hampers the spirit of even the fastest single processor setup.
key_insight: The real value of multi-processor systems lies not in raw benchmark speed but in eliminating the costly context-switching overhead that cripples single-processor machines during everyday multitasking.
